title: Shchi
subtitle: Russian cabbage soup
excerpt: A clear beef broth with fresh cabbage, potato, carrot, and onion, Russia's oldest and most universal soup. Plain, sustaining, and served with a large spoonful of soured cream stirred in at the table.
sourceNotes: Shchi (cabbage soup) has been the everyday soup of the Russian table for at least a thousand years and is mentioned in chronicles from the eleventh century. It exists in two principal forms: fresh shchi (from fresh cabbage, made year-round) and grey or sour shchi (from sauerkraut, made in winter). This is the fresh version. The defining characteristic of shchi is its simplicity, cabbage, broth, root vegetables, and nothing else except soured cream at the table. Meat in the broth is traditional but the soup works equally well without it.

content.text: Shchi is deliberately plain. It is not a flavour-layered dish: it is cabbage in good broth, with potato and carrot for body and a spoonful of soured cream to enrich each bowl at the table. The simplicity is the point. Made with decent stock (ideally from beef bones or a leftover roast), it is deeply satisfying without being at all complicated.

The cabbage should be shredded medium-fine and added towards the end of cooking so it keeps some texture. Overcooked cabbage in shchi becomes grey and bitter; properly cooked cabbage is tender but still faintly green.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: peeled and diced into 2 cm cubes

content.text: Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and {{carrot}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Stir in the {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{stock-beef}} and bring to a simmer. Add the {{potato}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{allspice}}. Simmer for 15 minutes.

Add the {{cabbage-white}} and simmer for a further 10 to 12 minutes until the potato is completely tender and the cabbage is soft but not grey. Remove the bay leaves and allspice berries. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Ladle into bowls, scatter with {{dill}}, and serve with a generous spoonful of {{soured-cream}} at the table.

items.fix: Add the cabbage only in the last 10 to 12 minutes of cooking. It should be tender but retain its pale green colour
Add another teaspoon of sea salt and taste. A second tablespoon of tomato puree, cooked out in the butter with the onion at the start, makes a substantial difference to the broth depth
items.cause: Not enough seasoning or tomato puree

items.fix: Add the potato at the right time and test it frequently after 15 minutes. It should be tender, not falling apart. Waxy potato holds up better if disintegration is a recurring problem

content.text: Shchi is Russia's daily bread in soup form. Pushkin wrote about it, Tolstoy ate it, and the proverb says: 'shchi da kasha, pishcha nasha' (cabbage soup and porridge: that is our food). It has been made with the same basic ingredients (cabbage, broth, whatever root vegetables were in the cellar) for a thousand years. The winter version made with sauerkraut (kislye shchi, sour shchi) is more complex and more intensely flavoured; this summer version with fresh cabbage is lighter and quicker. Both are served with soured cream and bread.
